ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper provides a systematic, cross-linguistic empirical comparison of subword units and composition functions (ten models; Table 2 / reimplementation in a single TensorFlow framework), and identifies a previously unstudied combination—character-trigram representations composed with bi-LSTMs—that outperforms most alternatives. It also uses human-annotated morphological analyses (Section 5.1) to show that character/unsupervised segmentation do not fully replace gold morphology. However, the work is primarily an empirical evaluation and does not propose a new algorithmic method or theoretical framework, so its novelty is moderate rather than high.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a generally logical structure (representation models → language model → training → experiments) and key components (σ, f, equations 1, 8–10) are introduced, but presentation is marred by many formatting and repetition issues (large blocks of duplicated sentences and references, missing subsection 3.2, and unusable repeated text). Notation is mostly defined (σ, Ws, f, LSTM states) but some sentences are awkward or confusing (e.g. “making design it possible”) and references to figures/tables (Figure 1, Tables 3/6/10/11) appear without context here. Overall readable but several sections and the manuscript layout need cleanup to remove duplications, fix numbering, and improve grammatical clarity.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Yes — the paper studies core NLP/ML concerns (subword representations and morphology) using standard neural methods and evaluation appropriate for top venues: it compares character/char-trigram/morph/BPE units and composition functions (bi-LSTM, CNN, addition) on an LSTM language modeling task using perplexity (Sections 3 and 3.3, 4.1). The multi-language/typology focus and inclusion of human-annotated morphological analyses for Czech and Russian (Section 5.1) clearly target the NLP research community, and the work is positioned against relevant prior work (Ling et al., Kim et al., Sennrich et al.) making it a strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles a central practical problem—how subword representations capture morphology across typologically diverse languages—by systematically comparing units (characters, char-trigrams, morphs/BPE) and composition functions (bi-LSTM, CNN, addition) on language modeling (Sections 3, 4). It produces actionable findings (char-trigram + bi-LSTM often best; character models are strong but fail to match models with gold morphological analyses; unsupervised segmentation is an inadequate substitute; Table 6 and qualitative analyses), which should influence representation choices and motivate work on improved unsupervised morphology and handling non-concatenative processes. These results are likely to be widely used by multilingual NLP researchers and open clear follow-up directions, giving the work strong but not fully transformative impact.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares ten varied models including recent baselines (e.g., Ling et al. 2015; Kim et al. 2016; Miyamoto & Cho 2016; Sennrich et al. 2016; Bojanowski et al. 2016) and evaluates them across multiple languages and typologies (sec. 3, related citations). Comparisons are made under controlled, fair conditions: all models were reimplemented in a single TensorFlow framework, use the same datasets and ~1.2M/150K splits, and the same evaluation metric (test perplexity) with the output vocabulary limited to the most frequent 5,000 words to match Ling et al. (2015). The paper acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably or better (e.g., reproducing that character bi-LSTMs outperform word models; and showing hand-annotated morphological analyses outperform character/unsupervised segmentation, sec. 5.1 and prelim. experiments). It also analyzes differences qualitatively and cites and discusses the most relevant competing approaches throughout (numerous specific prior works are referenced).

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main empirical claims are supported by systematic experiments: they reimplemented competing models in a single TensorFlow framework, used comparable datasets (~1.2M train / 150K dev/test) and hyperparameters, and report that a character-trigram + bi-LSTM composition often outperforms other subword choices; Table 6 further supports the claim that gold morphological analyses (Czech, Russian) give substantially better perplexities than unsupervised/character models. The mathematical formulation (LM and composition equations) is standard and correct. Minor technical concerns that do not overturn the conclusions are the lack of reported statistical significance tests, the evaluation restriction to a fixed 5,000-word output vocabulary (which they note may limit open-vocabulary comparison), and that gold-morphology experiments are shown for only two languages, limiting generality.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper runs a broad set of experiments comparing ten representation variants (subword units and composition functions) across multiple languages/typologies, includes quantitative LM results (including gold morphological analyses for Czech and Russian), and provides targeted qualitative analyses (nearest neighbors, reduplication) that support the main claims. It also documents model architectures, training hyperparameters, BPE/Morfessor settings, dataset sizes, and preprocessing choices and reimplements all models in a single framework, which aids reproducibility. Missing are more systematic sensitivity/ablation studies over key design choices (e.g., BPE merge count, output-vocabulary size, hyperparameter robustness) and only two languages have gold morphological annotations, which limits the strength of the claim about manual analyses vs. character/unsupervised methods.
